verificationNote: Based on CSA Enterprise Architecture guidance, NIST SP 800-210 (General Access Control Guidance for Cloud Systems), AWS Well-Architected Framework, Azure Cloud Adoption Framework, and Google Cloud Architecture Framework. Layer definitions synthesised from common patterns across all major CSPs.
description: A layered reference architecture for cloud environments, analogous to the Purdue Model for OT environments. This model defines seven functional layers — from the internet-facing edge to the management plane — each with distinct security characteristics, attack surfaces, and control requirements. Unlike the Purdue Model's strict hierarchical data flow, cloud architecture allows and requires cross-layer communication through APIs, IAM, and service mesh — making identity the primary security boundary rather than network segmentation alone.
modernConsiderations: Cloud architecture is API-driven and identity-centric. Network boundaries still matter (VPCs, subnets, security groups) but identity and access management is the primary control plane. Zero-trust principles apply: never trust network location alone, always verify identity, enforce least privilege, and assume breach. Service mesh and API gateways replace traditional firewalls as the primary traffic control mechanism.

layers.name: Edge / CDN Layer
layers.description: The internet-facing edge of the cloud architecture. Content delivery networks, DNS, and DDoS protection services that sit in front of all customer-facing resources. This layer absorbs volumetric attacks, terminates TLS, caches static content, and routes traffic to the application layer. It is the first line of defence and the first point of contact for all external traffic.

securityCharacteristics.attackSurface: Directly internet-facing — exposed to the full spectrum of internet threats including DDoS, DNS hijacking, BGP manipulation, TLS downgrade attacks, and web scraping
securityCharacteristics.vulnerabilities: Misconfigured CDN caching sensitive content, TLS certificate mismanagement, DNS zone transfer exposure, CDN origin IP leakage allowing attackers to bypass CDN protections, subdomain takeover via dangling DNS records
primaryControls: TLS 1.2+ enforcement with modern cipher suites
DNSSEC for DNS integrity
DDoS protection enabled for all public endpoints
Origin cloaking — CDN should be the only path to origin servers
Certificate automation and monitoring (CT logs)
Regular subdomain audit to prevent takeover

layers.name: Web / Application Layer
layers.description: Load balancers, API gateways, and web application firewalls that receive traffic from the edge layer and distribute it to compute resources. This layer handles request routing, SSL termination, authentication enforcement, rate limiting, and application-level security filtering. It is the primary enforcement point for API security policies.

securityCharacteristics.attackSurface: Application-layer attacks (OWASP Top 10), API abuse, authentication bypass, SSRF through web applications, request smuggling, GraphQL introspection and batching attacks
securityCharacteristics.vulnerabilities: Misconfigured WAF rules (bypass via encoding or HTTP/2), API gateway allowing unauthenticated access, load balancer health check endpoints exposing internal state, overly permissive CORS policies, missing rate limiting enabling credential stuffing
primaryControls: WAF with managed rule sets (OWASP CRS) plus custom rules
API authentication on all endpoints (OAuth 2.0, API keys, mutual TLS)
Rate limiting and throttling per client
Request validation (schema validation, input sanitisation)
mTLS between services via service mesh
Health check endpoints restricted to internal networks

layers.name: Compute Layer
layers.description: The processing tier where application code executes — virtual machines, containers, and serverless functions. This layer is the primary target for attackers seeking code execution, lateral movement, and data access. Security characteristics vary dramatically between VMs (full OS attack surface), containers (shared kernel), and serverless (ephemeral, no OS access).

securityCharacteristics.attackSurface: VM: full OS attack surface (unpatched CVEs, privilege escalation, credential theft via instance metadata). Container: container escape, vulnerable base images, Kubernetes API exploitation, pod-to-pod lateral movement. Serverless: function code vulnerabilities, event injection, overprivileged execution roles
securityCharacteristics.vulnerabilities: Instance metadata service (IMDS) v1 allowing SSRF-based credential theft (Capital One attack vector), Kubernetes dashboard exposed without authentication, container images with known CVEs, privileged containers with host namespace access, function roles with excessive permissions, SSH keys shared across instances
primaryControls: IMDSv2 enforcement (AWS) / metadata concealment (GCP) / IMDS restrictions (Azure)
CIS Benchmark hardening for VM images
Container image scanning in CI/CD and registry
Pod security standards (restricted profile) in Kubernetes
Least-privilege IAM roles for all compute (one role per workload)
No SSH/RDP in production — use SSM Session Manager or serial console
Runtime security monitoring (Falco, GuardDuty Runtime Monitoring)

layers.name: Data Layer
layers.description: Databases, object storage, data warehouses, and caching services. This layer contains the organisation's most valuable asset — its data. Security at this layer focuses on encryption, access control, data classification, and preventing unauthorised data exfiltration. Every cloud security breach ultimately targets this layer.

examples: AWS RDS (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server)

securityCharacteristics.attackSurface: Public bucket/blob exposure, SQL injection through application layer, database credential theft, data exfiltration through authorised API calls, snapshot/backup exposure, cross-account data sharing misconfiguration
securityCharacteristics.vulnerabilities: Publicly accessible S3 buckets (the single most common cloud misconfiguration), database instances with public endpoints, unencrypted database snapshots shared across accounts, overly permissive IAM policies granting broad data access, missing VPC endpoints causing data to traverse the internet, Snowflake accounts without MFA (2024 breaches)
primaryControls: Block public access at account/organisation level (S3 Block Public Access, Azure storage account settings)
Encryption at rest with customer-managed keys (CMK) via KMS
Encryption in transit (TLS) for all database connections
VPC endpoints / private endpoints for all data services — no public internet exposure
Data classification and tagging (Macie, Purview, SCC)
Database audit logging enabled and forwarded to SIEM
Backup encryption and access controls matching production data

layers.name: Identity Layer
layers.description: The identity and access management layer spans all other layers — it is the control plane for who can do what to which resource. In cloud environments, identity is the new perimeter. A compromised identity with broad permissions is more dangerous than a network breach. This layer includes identity providers, SSO, MFA, privileged access management, and service-to-service authentication.

securityCharacteristics.attackSurface: Credential theft (phishing, infostealers, dark web credential dumps), MFA bypass (MFA fatigue, SIM swapping, adversary-in-the-middle), session hijacking, OAuth token theft, SAML assertion forging (SolarWinds Golden SAML), service account key exposure
securityCharacteristics.vulnerabilities: No MFA enforcement (single biggest identity risk), overprivileged service accounts and roles, stale credentials not rotated, long-lived API keys, shared service account credentials, federated identity trust misconfiguration, insufficient conditional access policies
primaryControls: MFA enforcement for all human users — phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/WebAuthn) preferred
Conditional access policies (device compliance, location, risk-based)
Just-in-time privileged access with approval workflows
No long-lived access keys — use short-lived credentials (STS, managed identities, workload identity)
Regular access reviews with automated revocation
Service account key rotation or elimination (use workload identity federation)
Identity threat detection (Entra ID Protection, GuardDuty IAM findings)

layers.name: Network Layer
layers.description: Virtual network infrastructure including VPCs/VNets, subnets, security groups, firewalls, VPN gateways, and private connectivity. While identity is the primary security boundary in cloud, network segmentation remains essential for defence-in-depth. Network controls limit blast radius, enforce micro-segmentation, and prevent lateral movement even when identity controls are bypassed.

securityCharacteristics.attackSurface: Overly permissive security groups (0.0.0.0/0 on management ports), VPC peering without traffic filtering, public subnets with unnecessary internet exposure, DNS-based data exfiltration, VPN credential compromise for hybrid connectivity
securityCharacteristics.vulnerabilities: Security groups allowing SSH/RDP from anywhere, public subnets containing databases or backend services, missing VPC Flow Logs, no DNS logging, transit gateway with flat routing allowing full inter-VPC lateral movement, Network ACLs ignored or set to allow-all
primaryControls: Deny-all default security groups — explicit permit only
Private subnets for all non-edge resources
VPC Flow Logs enabled and forwarded to SIEM
DNS logging enabled (Route 53 Resolver Query Logging, VPC DNS)
VPC endpoints for all AWS service access (eliminate internet dependency)
Network segmentation by workload/environment (separate VPCs for production/staging)
Micro-segmentation via security groups at instance level

layers.name: Management / Control Plane Layer
layers.description: The management plane — the API surface through which all cloud resources are created, configured, monitored, and destroyed. This is the most critical layer because compromise of the management plane enables an attacker to modify any resource in any other layer. Includes cloud console access, CLI access, infrastructure-as-code pipelines, logging, monitoring, and CSPM.

examples: Azure Activity Log / Entra Audit Logs

securityCharacteristics.attackSurface: Cloud console credential theft, API key compromise, IaC pipeline injection (supply chain), Terraform state file exposure (contains secrets), CloudTrail tampering or deletion, organisation admin account takeover
securityCharacteristics.vulnerabilities: Root/global admin accounts without MFA, CloudTrail disabled or logs deleted by attacker, Terraform state files stored in unencrypted S3 buckets, IaC pipelines with overprivileged service principals, CSPM findings ignored or not triaged, drift between IaC definition and actual state allowing shadow configuration
primaryControls: Root account MFA + no routine use (AWS Organizations root)
CloudTrail enabled in all regions with log file integrity validation
Terraform state encrypted and access-controlled (S3 + DynamoDB lock with KMS encryption)
IaC pipeline least-privilege — CI/CD service principals scoped to specific resources
Service Control Policies / Organization Policies as guardrails (preventive controls)
CSPM with automated remediation for critical findings
Break-glass procedures for emergency admin access (documented, audited, rarely used)
